What kind of data was the system trained.question_interpretation: This asks what type of information the AI system uses as input — what was collected, from whom, and in what format — so that clinicians can judge whether the data is relevant to their patient population.
What kind of data was the system trained.answer: The system was trained on structured survey data from the National 2009 H1N1 Flu Survey (NHFS). The dataset contains 35 input features covering respondents' demographic characteristics (age group, sex, ethnicity, education, income, marital status, employment), health-related factors (chronic medical conditions, health insurance status, healthcare worker status, contact with children under six months), behavioural factors (hand washing, face mask use, avoidance of large gatherings, antiviral medication use), and opinion-based factors (perceived vaccine effectiveness, perceived risk of illness, worry about vaccine side effects). The data also includes geographic information (HHS region and metropolitan statistical area classification). All features are either categorical or ordinal integer-coded variables. The two target variables are binary indicators of whether the respondent received the H1N1 vaccine and/or the seasonal flu vaccine.

What is the source of the training data?.question_interpretation: This asks where the data originated and who collected it, which is important for assessing data quality, governance, and whether the population surveyed is representative of the patients you serve.
What is the source of the training data?.answer: The data comes from the National 2009 H1N1 Flu Survey (NHFS), which was sponsored by the National Center for Immunization and Respiratory Diseases (NCIRD) and conducted jointly by NCIRD and the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), both part of the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). The NHFS was a list-assisted random-digit-dialling telephone survey of households conducted between October 2009 and June 2010, designed to monitor influenza immunisation coverage during the 2009-10 season. The dataset was accessed via the DrivenData competition 'Flu Shot Learning: Predict H1N1 and Seasonal Flu Vaccines'.

How were the labels/ground-truth produce.question_interpretation: This asks how the outcome the system is trying to predict — whether someone received a vaccine — was determined, which matters for understanding the reliability of the 'correct answer' the system learns from.
How were the labels/ground-truth produce.answer: The labels are self-reported vaccination status. Respondents in the NHFS telephone survey were asked whether they had received the H1N1 flu vaccine and whether they had received the seasonal flu vaccine. Each response was recorded as a binary value (0 = No, 1 = Yes). Because these labels rely on self-report rather than verified medical records, there is an inherent risk of recall bias or social desirability bias, which clinicians should bear in mind when interpreting the system's predictions.

What is the sample size of the training.question_interpretation: This asks how many individual records the system learned from, which helps clinicians assess whether the dataset is large enough to produce reliable and generalisable predictions.
What is the sample size of the training.answer: The full dataset contains 26,707 respondents with 37 columns (35 input features and 2 target variables). The data was split into training and test sets for model development. The model used for explainability and deployment was evaluated on a test set of 5,342 observations. The target population for the NHFS was all persons aged 6 months or older living in the United States at the time of interview.

What dataset(s) is the system NOT using?.question_interpretation: This asks what potentially relevant information is absent from the system, helping clinicians understand blind spots and what the AI cannot account for in its predictions.
What dataset(s) is the system NOT using?.answer: The system does not use clinical or medical record data such as laboratory results, diagnoses, prescriptions, or hospital encounter data. It does not incorporate real-time epidemiological surveillance data, geographic granularity beyond HHS region and metropolitan statistical area classification, or individual-level clinical risk scores. The seasonal flu opinion variables (opinion_seas_vacc_effective, opinion_seas_risk, opinion_seas_sick_from_vacc) and the seasonal_vaccine target were dropped when modelling H1N1 vaccine uptake specifically. Additionally, the employment_industry and employment_occupation fields contain high proportions of missing data (approximately 50%) and are anonymised with coded labels, limiting their interpretability.

What are the potential limitations/biase.question_interpretation: This asks what systematic issues in the data could lead the AI system to produce skewed or unfair predictions, which is critical for ensuring equitable and safe use in clinical or public health settings.
What are the potential limitations/biase.answer: Several limitations and biases should be noted. First, the data is based on self-reported telephone surveys, which introduces recall bias and may exclude populations without telephone access (e.g. homeless individuals, those in institutional settings). Second, there is significant missing data across several features — notably health_insurance (approximately 46% missing), employment_industry and employment_occupation (approximately 50% missing), and doctor_recc_h1n1 (approximately 8% missing). Third, the data reflects the U.S. population during the 2009-2010 H1N1 pandemic, so it may not generalise to other countries, time periods, or pandemic contexts. Fourth, the H1N1 vaccine uptake rate is imbalanced (approximately 21% received the vaccine versus 79% who did not), which can affect the system's ability to correctly identify individuals who will accept vaccination. Fifth, demographic variables such as race, sex, and income are included, raising fairness considerations about whether the system may perform differently across subgroups.

What kind of output does the system give.question_interpretation: This asks what form the AI system's prediction takes — a probability, a category, or a score — so clinicians know how to read and interpret the result.
What kind of output does the system give.answer: The system produces two types of output for each individual: a predicted probability (a continuous value between 0 and 1 indicating the likelihood that the respondent received the H1N1 vaccine) and a predicted binary classification (0 or 1, indicating 'not vaccinated' or 'vaccinated'). The binary classification is derived from the probability using a threshold (typically 0.5). This means clinicians receive both a nuanced risk estimate and a clear yes/no prediction.

What does the system output mean?.question_interpretation: This asks how to interpret the AI's prediction in practical terms — what a high or low score signifies for a given individual in a public health context.
What does the system output mean?.answer: A predicted probability close to 1.0 indicates the system believes the individual is very likely to have accepted (or to accept) the H1N1 vaccine, based on their demographic profile, health behaviours, opinions, and whether their doctor recommended vaccination. A probability close to 0.0 suggests the individual is unlikely to have been vaccinated. For example, a prediction of 0.206 for a given respondent means the system estimates roughly a 21% chance that person received the H1N1 vaccine. This output can help public health professionals identify population segments that may benefit from targeted vaccination outreach.

What is the scope of the system's capabi.question_interpretation: This asks what the system can and cannot do, so clinicians do not overestimate its abilities or use it for purposes it was not designed for.
What is the scope of the system's capabi.answer: The system is designed to predict H1N1 vaccine uptake based on survey-derived features. It can estimate the likelihood that an individual with a given profile received the H1N1 vaccine during the 2009-2010 season. It can also support what-if analyses, allowing users to explore how changing specific factors (e.g. doctor recommendation, opinion on vaccine effectiveness) might alter the predicted probability. The system cannot diagnose illness, recommend treatment, predict clinical outcomes, or forecast future pandemic vaccine uptake in real time. It is a retrospective model trained on historical survey data and should not be used as a sole basis for individual clinical decisions.

How is the output used for other system.question_interpretation: This asks whether the AI's prediction feeds into other automated systems or workflows, which matters for understanding downstream effects and accountability.
How is the output used for other system.answer: The system's predicted probabilities are used as input to the explainability and what-if analysis components. The explainability module uses the model's predictions to compute feature importance rankings, partial dependence profiles, and individual-level breakdowns that show which factors drove a particular prediction. The what-if dashboard allows users to modify input features and observe how the predicted probability changes. There is no information in the available documentation about integration with other clinical or operational systems beyond these analytical components.

How should I best utilize the output of.question_interpretation: This asks for practical guidance on how healthcare professionals should use the AI's predictions responsibly and effectively in their work.
How should I best utilize the output of.answer: The system's output is best used as a decision-support tool for public health planning rather than for individual patient-level clinical decisions. Healthcare professionals can use the predicted probabilities to identify population subgroups with low predicted vaccine uptake, enabling targeted outreach campaigns. The what-if analysis feature is particularly useful for exploring hypothetical scenarios — for instance, understanding how much predicted uptake might increase if doctor recommendations were more widespread. Clinicians should always combine the system's output with their own professional judgement, local epidemiological knowledge, and patient-specific factors that the model may not capture.

How should the output fit in my workflow.question_interpretation: This asks where in a clinical or public health workflow the AI's predictions should be consulted, and how they should complement existing processes.
How should the output fit in my workflow.answer: The system is best positioned as a population-level screening and planning tool within public health workflows. It could be used during the planning phase of vaccination campaigns to stratify target populations by predicted uptake likelihood, helping allocate resources to communities or demographic groups where uptake is predicted to be low. It should not replace individual patient consultations or clinical assessments. The what-if dashboard can be used in policy planning meetings to model the potential impact of interventions such as increasing doctor recommendations or addressing vaccine hesitancy concerns.

What is the source of the information ob.question_interpretation: This asks where the data behind each prediction comes from, so clinicians can assess the provenance and trustworthiness of the information.
What is the source of the information ob.answer: All predictions are derived from the National 2009 H1N1 Flu Survey (NHFS) data, collected by the CDC through random-digit-dialling telephone interviews. The input features used for prediction come directly from respondents' self-reported answers about their demographics, health behaviours, opinions on vaccines, and whether their doctor recommended vaccination. No external clinical data sources, electronic health records, or real-time surveillance feeds are used.

What is the scope of the output data?.question_interpretation: This asks what population and context the system's predictions apply to, helping clinicians understand the boundaries of generalisability.
What is the scope of the output data?.answer: The system's predictions are scoped to the U.S. population aged 6 months and older during the 2009-2010 H1N1 flu season. The model was trained on a nationally representative telephone survey sample. Predictions may not generalise to populations outside the United States, to different pandemic or seasonal flu contexts, or to time periods with different public health messaging, vaccine availability, or societal attitudes towards vaccination.

What is the amount of the output data?.question_interpretation: This asks how many predictions the system produces and in what volume, which is relevant for understanding workload and data management.
What is the amount of the output data?.answer: The system produces one prediction per individual respondent. For each person, it outputs a single predicted probability and a corresponding binary classification for H1N1 vaccine uptake. In the test set, this amounted to 5,342 individual predictions. The system can generate predictions for any new individual whose feature values are provided in the same format as the training data.

What logic is used for the initial outpu.question_interpretation: This asks what reasoning process the AI follows to arrive at its prediction, so clinicians can understand the basis for the system's recommendations.
What logic is used for the initial outpu.answer: The system uses an XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) classifier, which builds an ensemble of decision trees sequentially. Each tree learns to correct the errors of the previous ones. The model processes input features through a preprocessing pipeline that imputes missing numerical values using the most frequent value and handles categorical variables through one-hot encoding. The final prediction is a weighted combination of all trees' outputs, converted to a probability via a logistic function. The most influential features driving predictions include the respondent's opinion on H1N1 vaccine effectiveness, whether a doctor recommended the H1N1 vaccine, and the respondent's perceived risk of H1N1 illness.

How accurate/precise/reliable are the pr.question_interpretation: This asks how well the system's predictions match reality, which is essential for clinicians to know how much confidence to place in the output.
How accurate/precise/reliable are the pr.answer: The system achieved a best ROC AUC score of 0.837 during cross-validation, indicating good discriminative ability — that is, the system is reasonably effective at distinguishing between individuals who did and did not receive the H1N1 vaccine. Multiple algorithms were benchmarked, and the selected XGBoost model performed competitively alongside logistic regression, random forest, and neural network models, all of which achieved similar accuracy levels. However, clinicians should note that an AUC of 0.837 means the system is not perfect and will produce both false positives and false negatives.

How often does the system make mistakes?.question_interpretation: This asks about the system's error rate, helping clinicians understand how frequently the AI gets it wrong and what the consequences might be.
How often does the system make mistakes?.answer: Based on the model's performance metrics, the system makes incorrect predictions in a meaningful proportion of cases. Given the class imbalance in the data (approximately 21% vaccinated versus 79% not vaccinated), the system is more likely to correctly identify individuals who were not vaccinated than those who were. The specific false positive and false negative rates are not explicitly reported in the available documentation, but the ROC AUC of 0.837 suggests that while the system performs well overall, clinicians should expect some misclassifications, particularly among borderline cases where the predicted probability is close to the decision threshold.

In what situations is the system likely.question_interpretation: This asks under what conditions the AI performs well or poorly, so clinicians can calibrate their trust depending on the patient or population context.
In what situations is the system likely.answer: The system is likely to be most accurate for individuals with clear-cut profiles — for example, someone whose doctor recommended the H1N1 vaccine, who believes the vaccine is very effective, and who perceives high personal risk of H1N1 illness is very likely to be correctly predicted as vaccinated. Conversely, the system may struggle with individuals who have mixed or contradictory profiles (e.g. high concern about H1N1 but also high worry about vaccine side effects). The system may also be less reliable for subgroups underrepresented in the training data or for individuals with high proportions of missing feature values, particularly health insurance status and employment-related fields.

What are the limitations of the system?.question_interpretation: This asks about the system's known weaknesses and constraints, which is critical for safe deployment and for understanding when not to rely on the AI.
What are the limitations of the system?.answer: Key limitations include: the model is trained on retrospective 2009-2010 U.S. survey data and may not reflect current attitudes, vaccine availability, or public health contexts; the data is self-reported and subject to recall and social desirability biases; significant missing data exists across several features (notably health insurance at ~46% missing); the class imbalance (only ~21% vaccinated) may reduce the system's sensitivity for identifying vaccine acceptors; the model does not account for clinical variables, comorbidity details, or real-time epidemiological conditions; and some features (employment industry and occupation) are anonymised, limiting clinical interpretability.

What kind of mistakes is the system like.question_interpretation: This asks about the types of errors the system tends to produce, which helps clinicians anticipate and mitigate potential harms from incorrect predictions.
What kind of mistakes is the system like.answer: Given the class imbalance (approximately 79% of respondents were not vaccinated), the system is more likely to incorrectly predict that someone was not vaccinated when they actually were (i.e. missing true vaccine acceptors) than the reverse. In public health terms, this means the system may underestimate vaccine uptake in certain subgroups, potentially leading to over-allocation of outreach resources to groups that are already well-covered. Conversely, it may occasionally predict vaccination for individuals who did not actually receive the vaccine, which could lead to under-targeting of genuinely hesitant populations.

How does the system make predictions?.question_interpretation: This asks about the overall mechanism the AI uses to arrive at its predictions, explained in terms that help clinicians understand the reasoning process.
How does the system make predictions?.answer: The system uses an XGBoost classifier, which is an ensemble machine learning method that builds many small decision trees in sequence. Each tree focuses on correcting the mistakes of the previous trees. Think of it like a panel of specialists, each refining the assessment of the previous one. The model takes in a respondent's survey answers — their demographics, health behaviours, opinions about vaccines, and whether their doctor recommended vaccination — processes them through a preprocessing pipeline, and produces a probability estimate of H1N1 vaccine uptake. The final prediction is the combined output of all the decision trees.

What features does the system consider?.question_interpretation: This asks which specific pieces of information the AI uses as inputs, so clinicians know what factors are driving the predictions.
What features does the system consider?.answer: The system considers 31 input features (after dropping the seasonal vaccine target and seasonal opinion variables for the H1N1 model). These include: opinion-based features (perceived H1N1 vaccine effectiveness, perceived H1N1 risk, worry about getting sick from the H1N1 vaccine); doctor recommendation (whether a doctor recommended the H1N1 and seasonal vaccines); behavioural features (antiviral medication use, avoidance behaviours, face mask use, hand washing, reducing large gatherings, reducing outside-home contact, avoiding touching face); health factors (chronic medical conditions, health insurance, healthcare worker status, contact with children under 6 months); and demographic features (age group, education, race, sex, income relative to poverty level, marital status, housing status, employment status, geographic region, metropolitan area classification, household composition).

What is the system's overall logic?.question_interpretation: This asks for a high-level summary of how the system reasons, helping clinicians form a mental model of the AI's decision-making approach.
What is the system's overall logic?.answer: At a high level, the system's logic can be summarised as follows: individuals who believe the H1N1 vaccine is effective, whose doctor recommended vaccination, and who perceive a higher personal risk from H1N1 are predicted to have a substantially higher probability of vaccine uptake. Behavioural factors such as proactive health measures (hand washing, avoiding gatherings) also contribute positively. Demographic factors such as age, healthcare worker status, and health insurance coverage further refine the prediction. The system essentially learns the statistical patterns that distinguish vaccine acceptors from non-acceptors across these dimensions.

How does it weigh different features?.question_interpretation: This asks about the relative importance of different input factors, so clinicians understand which variables have the greatest influence on the AI's predictions.
How does it weigh different features?.answer: Permutation-based variable importance analysis reveals that opinion-related features are the most influential group, followed by doctor recommendation variables. Among individual features, opinion on H1N1 vaccine effectiveness is the single most important predictor. The features are grouped into four categories with the following importance ranking: (1) opinion variables (highest impact on model performance when removed), (2) doctor recommendation variables, (3) demographic/protected variables, and (4) behavioural variables (lowest individual impact). This means the system weighs a person's beliefs about vaccine effectiveness and their doctor's recommendation more heavily than their demographic background or health behaviours.

What kind of rules does it follow?.question_interpretation: This asks whether the system follows explicit, interpretable rules, which helps clinicians understand how transparent and auditable the AI's logic is.
What kind of rules does it follow?.answer: The XGBoost model does not follow a single set of explicit if-then rules in the way a simple decision tree would. Instead, it combines hundreds of small decision trees, each capturing a partial pattern in the data. However, the system's behaviour can be approximated through explainability tools: partial dependence profiles show how the predicted probability changes as a single feature varies (e.g. as opinion on vaccine effectiveness increases from 'not at all effective' to 'very effective', the predicted probability of vaccination rises substantially). Individual predictions can be broken down to show which features pushed the prediction higher or lower for a specific person.

What are the top rules/features that det.question_interpretation: This asks which factors matter most in driving the AI's predictions, giving clinicians a clear sense of the key determinants.
What are the top rules/features that det.answer: The top features determining predictions, based on permutation variable importance, are: (1) opinion on H1N1 vaccine effectiveness — the single most influential feature; (2) doctor recommendation for H1N1 vaccine; (3) opinion on H1N1 risk (perceived likelihood of getting sick without the vaccine); (4) opinion on getting sick from the H1N1 vaccine (vaccine safety concerns); and (5) doctor recommendation for seasonal vaccine. Demographic features such as age group and healthcare worker status also contribute meaningfully. Behavioural features like hand washing and face mask use have comparatively lower individual importance.

What kind of algorithm is used?.question_interpretation: This asks about the specific machine learning method employed, which is relevant for understanding the system's complexity, transparency, and known strengths or weaknesses.
What kind of algorithm is used?.answer: The system uses XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting), a gradient-boosted decision tree algorithm. This was selected after benchmarking against logistic regression, random forest, and a multi-layer perceptron (neural network). All four models achieved similar performance, but XGBoost was chosen for deployment. XGBoost is widely used in healthcare predictive modelling due to its strong performance on structured/tabular data, its ability to handle missing values, and its compatibility with explainability tools such as SHAP and DALEX.

How were the parameters set?.question_interpretation: This asks how the system's internal settings were configured, which affects model performance and whether the system was rigorously optimised.
How were the parameters set?.answer: The model's parameters were set using randomised search cross-validation with 5-fold cross-validation and 50 candidate parameter combinations (totalling 250 fits). The best parameters found were: subsample of 0.8, scale_pos_weight of 1, regularisation lambda of 100, regularisation alpha of 0.1, 200 estimators (trees), minimum child weight of 3, maximum depth of 3, learning rate of 0.01, gamma of 0.1, and colsample_bytree of 0.8. The optimisation metric was ROC AUC, achieving a best score of 0.837. This rigorous tuning process helps ensure the model generalises well to unseen data rather than overfitting to the training set.

How have updates affected the system?.question_interpretation: This asks whether the system has been modified since its initial development and how any changes have impacted its performance or behaviour.
How have updates affected the system?.answer: The available documentation does not describe any updates, versioning history, or iterative modifications to the system after its initial development. The model appears to have been developed as a single-iteration project based on the NHFS dataset. There is no information about retraining on newer data, model drift monitoring, or performance tracking over time. If the system were to be deployed in a live public health setting, a clear update and monitoring protocol would need to be established.

Does the system learn specifically from.question_interpretation: This asks whether the AI adapts or changes based on how individual users interact with it, which is important for understanding consistency and accountability.
Does the system learn specifically from.answer: No. The system does not learn from individual user interactions. It is a static, pre-trained model — once trained on the NHFS dataset, its parameters are fixed. The what-if dashboard allows users to explore hypothetical scenarios by modifying input values, but these interactions do not alter the underlying model. Every user querying the system with the same input values will receive the same prediction.

Why is the system using or not using a g.question_interpretation: This asks about the rationale behind specific design choices, helping clinicians understand whether the system was built thoughtfully and with appropriate justification.
Why is the system using or not using a g.answer: XGBoost was selected after benchmarking four candidate algorithms (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, and multi-layer perceptron) using cross-validated accuracy scores. All performed similarly, but XGBoost was chosen for deployment. The seasonal flu opinion variables were deliberately excluded from the H1N1 prediction model to avoid data leakage and to focus the model on H1N1-specific predictors. Features with very high missing data rates (such as health insurance at ~46%) were retained but imputed, which is a design choice that clinicians should be aware of as it may introduce noise. Employment industry and occupation were retained despite being anonymised.

What are the results of other people usi.question_interpretation: This asks whether there is evidence from other users or external validation that supports the system's reliability and usefulness.
What are the results of other people usi.answer: The available documentation does not include information about external validation studies, user feedback, or results from other institutions or practitioners using this system. The model was developed within an academic and research context at Freie Universität Berlin and the Robert Koch Institute. Before clinical or public health deployment, independent external validation on a separate dataset and user acceptance testing with healthcare professionals would be strongly recommended.

Who is responsible for this system? Who.question_interpretation: This asks about accountability — who developed the system and who is responsible for its outputs, which is essential for governance and trust.
Who is responsible for this system? Who.answer: The system was developed by Henry El-Jawhari at Freie Universität (FU) Berlin and the Robert Koch Institute (RKI). The underlying data was collected by the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) through the National 2009 H1N1 Flu Survey. For questions about the system's design, validation, or appropriate use, the developing team at FU Berlin and RKI should be contacted. Responsibility for clinical decisions made using the system's output remains with the healthcare professional using it.

What are the affordances of the system?.question_interpretation: This asks what interactive capabilities the system offers to users, so clinicians know what they can do with it beyond simply viewing predictions.
What are the affordances of the system?.answer: The system provides several interactive analytical capabilities: (1) a what-if dashboard that allows users to modify individual input features and observe how the predicted probability of H1N1 vaccine uptake changes in real time; (2) variable importance visualisations showing which features matter most to the model overall; (3) partial dependence profiles illustrating how the predicted probability changes across the range of a given feature; and (4) individual prediction breakdowns that decompose a specific prediction into the contributions of each feature. These tools enable clinicians and public health professionals to explore, interrogate, and understand the model's behaviour rather than treating it as a black box.

What does this UI element do?.question_interpretation: This asks for clarification on specific interface components, so users can navigate and use the system effectively.
What does this UI element do?.answer: The what-if dashboard is the primary interactive interface. It displays individual respondent profiles with their feature values and predicted probability. Users can adjust feature values (e.g. change 'doctor_recc_h1n1' from 0 to 1, or modify 'opinion_h1n1_vacc_effective' to a higher value) and immediately see the updated prediction. The dashboard also shows the two most important features for each prediction alongside the probability score. The explainability visualisations include bar charts for variable importance (showing which features contribute most to model accuracy), line plots for partial dependence (showing the relationship between a feature and the predicted outcome), and breakdown plots for individual predictions (showing how each feature shifts the prediction above or below the average).
